beats.text: You learned to choose scales. You checked if the biggest number fits. You checked the last digits. Let's see what you know.

incorrects.description: Student selected Scale of 1—would need 71 tick marks to reach 70

params.description: Scale of 1 indicator highlights showing ❌ 'doesn't fit'.

steps.text: Scale of 1 would need 71 tick marks to reach Ben's 70 toys. That's too many lines on the axis. Which scale uses fewer marks?

incorrects.description: Student selected Scale of 2—would need 36 tick marks to reach 70

params.description: Scale of 2 indicator highlights showing ❌ 'doesn't fit'.

steps.text: Scale of 2 would need 36 tick marks to reach 70. That's still a lot of lines. Look for a bigger scale that fits the data with fewer marks.

incorrects.description: Student selected Scale of 5—would need 15 tick marks to reach 70

params.description: Scale of 5 indicator highlights showing ❌ 'doesn't fit'.

steps.text: Scale of 5 would need 15 tick marks. That works, but the data all ends in 0 or 5. Which bigger scale would be easiest to read?

incorrects.description: Student gave a wrong answer not covered by a specific Medium. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Visual sequence shows axis with each scale option. Scale of 1 displays crowded tick marks (0, 1, 2, 3... up to 70). Scale of 2 displays many marks (0, 2, 4, 6... up to 70). Scale of 5 displays moderate marks (0, 5, 10... up to 70). Scale of 10 displays clean axis (0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60, 70, 80) with 9 marks total. Scale of 10 option highlights with checkmark.

steps.text: Let me show you how to think about this. First, check the biggest number: Ben has 70 toys. Now count how many marks each scale needs. Scale of 1 needs 71 marks. Scale of 2 needs 36 marks. Scale of 5 needs 15 marks. Scale of 10 needs only 9 marks. All the values end in 0 or 5, so Scale of 10 shows everything exactly with the fewest marks. That is why Scale of 10 is the best choice.

incorrects.description: Student selected Scale of 5 — data values are not multiples of 5

params.description: All four data values highlight in the table.

steps.text: Look at the ones place in each number. Do they end in 0 or 5? Which scale shows ANY whole number exactly?

incorrects.description: Student selected Scale of 10 — scale too large for small data range

params.description: Hal's value (31, the maximum) highlights in the table.

steps.text: The biggest number is only 31. Scale of 10 would only show 0, 10, 20, 30. Where would 31 land? Which smaller scale can show exact values?

incorrects.description: Student gave a wrong answer not covered by a specific Medium. System models the correct answer.

params.description: All four values highlight in the data table.

steps.text: Let me walk you through the thinking. First, check the biggest number: 31. That fits with several scales. Next, look at the ones digits: 3, 1, 9, 6. None are 0 or 5, so these aren't multiples of 5. When numbers don't end in 0 or 5, Scale of 2 is the choice that shows every whole number exactly. The answer is Scale of 2 because it works for any whole number.

condition.selected: Scale of 5 — it shows more detail.

incorrects.description: Student selected Scale of 5 — works but inefficient given max value 90 and all values ending in 0

params.description: Vertical bar graph appears with Scale of 5. Axis 0–90 with 19 tick marks visible (0, 5, 10, 15...85, 90). All bars displayed.

steps.text: Scale of 5 works, but look at the axis. That's 19 tick marks to reach 90. All your numbers end in 0 or 5, so Scale of 10 would show them just as clearly with only 10 marks.

condition.selected: Scale of 2 — it works for any numbers.

incorrects.description: Student selected Scale of 2 — inefficient for max value 90

params.description: Vertical bar graph appears with Scale of 2. Axis 0–90 with 46 tick marks visible. All bars displayed but axis extremely crowded.

steps.text: Scale of 2 does work for any number, but it creates 46 tick marks to reach 90. That's too crowded. Since all values end in 0, Scale of 10 shows them exactly with far fewer marks.

incorrects.description: Student gave a wrong answer not covered by a specific Medium. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Vertical bar graph appears with Scale of 10. Axis 0–100 with 11 tick marks (0, 10, 20...90, 100). All bars land exactly on tick marks.

steps.text: Let me show you how to think about this. First, I check the biggest number — 90. That tells me the graph needs to reach at least 90. Then I look at all the values: 30, 70, 90, 50. Every one ends in 0 or 5, so they're multiples of 5. That means both Scale of 5 and Scale of 10 will show them exactly. Now I compare: Scale of 5 needs 19 tick marks to reach 90. Scale of 10 only needs 10. For data this big, Scale of 10 is easiest to read. The answer is Scale of 10 because it balances precision with clean visual design.
